foundational_seams.instance: A request enters the system via an HTTP/HTTPS browser session; the web application's entry page must be reachable and renderable in a standard browser without a native install.
The Freelancer entity is authenticated and a session is established; the system's core business rule that all access is gated by an authenticated freelancer identity is exercised.
The authenticated freelancer's identity (provider token and user profile reference) is stored and retrievable; state survives beyond the OAuth callback so the session can be resumed.

The system delegates authentication to an external OAuth provider; the OAuth handshake round-trip completes and the application obtains a valid authenticated session — no password is stored or managed internally.

skeleton.name: Sign in via OAuth on the web application
skeleton.retires_risk: OAuth provider integration (A2): first exercise of the external OAuth delegation; proves the provider round-trip works before any other slice depends on an authenticated session.

seam_coverage.establishes: The web application is reachable over HTTP/HTTPS in a standard browser and its entry page renders without a native install — proving the system's request-entry surface is alive and serving responses.

The core authentication business rule is exercised: a Freelancer initiates sign-in, the system delegates to an external OAuth provider, and an authenticated session exists in the application — proving the domain's identity gate operates correctly.

The provider token and user profile reference are stored and retrieved so the authenticated session persists in the application after the OAuth callback completes — proving state survives a round-trip through the data store.

The application completes an external OAuth handshake end-to-end — the Freelancer is redirected to the provider, grants authorisation, and the application receives and resolves the callback to an authenticated session with no password entry.

skeleton.minimal_behaviour_rationale: S1 carries only two requirements (R1, R5) and two acceptance criteria (AC1, AC5). The entire product surface beyond entry-page rendering and OAuth sign-in — project creation, time logging, invoice export, currency configuration, rate management — is deferred to later slices. No business data is created or displayed; the only state written is the minimal identity record (provider token and user profile reference) that proves the persistence seam. Everything else remains hardcoded or absent.
skeleton.selection_rationale: S1 is the only eligible slice that crosses all four foundational seams in a single end-to-end path. S2, S3, and S4 all miss the primary_external_integration seam — none of their requirements or acceptance criteria exercise the external OAuth handshake. S1 also sits at the root of the dependency graph (depends_on is empty); every other slice depends on S1 directly or transitively. Its retires_risk explicitly names the OAuth provider integration (A2) — the riskiest unproven foundational dependency, which gates every other slice. Among the eligible slices, S1 carries the least feature depth, making it the thinnest possible end-to-end proof of composition.

skeleton_seams.must_establish: The web application accepts HTTP/HTTPS requests in a standard browser and serves its entry page — the request-entry surface is live and functional.

The authentication business rule operates end-to-end: a Freelancer can initiate sign-in, the system applies the OAuth delegation rule, and a valid authenticated session is established in the application.

The Freelancer's identity (provider token and user profile reference) is written to and read from the data store; the authenticated session survives the OAuth callback and is retrievable on subsequent requests.

The application completes a full external OAuth provider handshake — initiating the redirect, receiving the callback, and resolving it to an authenticated session — proving the external delegation round-trip works before any other slice depends on it.

rejected_as_skeleton.name: Log and persist billable time entries against a project
rejected_as_skeleton.reason: Misses the primary_external_integration seam — neither R2/R7 nor AC2/AC7 exercise the external OAuth handshake. Also not the dependency root: depends_on includes S1 and S4, meaning it cannot be built first.

rejected_as_skeleton.name: Export a monthly PDF invoice with line items and monetary totals
rejected_as_skeleton.reason: Misses the primary_external_integration seam — none of its requirements (R3, R8, R9, R10) or acceptance criteria (AC3, AC8, AC9, AC10) exercise the external OAuth handshake. Deepest node in the dependency graph (depends_on: S1, S2, S4) and carries the most feature depth of any eligible slice.

rejected_as_skeleton.name: Create and manage client projects with currency and billable rate
rejected_as_skeleton.reason: Misses the primary_external_integration seam — neither R4/R6/R9/R10 nor AC4/AC6 exercise the external OAuth handshake. Also not the dependency root: depends_on includes S1.
